content.text: Every HTML page that a browser renders is built on a standard document structure. Understanding this structure is the first step toward writing valid, accessible HTML — and toward understanding what Next.js generates for you automatically. This sub-subtopic covers the mandatory skeleton of every HTML document, the purpose of each element it contains, and explains how Next.js controls that skeleton through its App Router and Pages Router APIs.

attrs.alt: HTML document tree: DOCTYPE → html → head (meta, title, link) and body (header, main, footer)

content.text: The very first line of every HTML file must be the document type declaration:
This single line tells the browser to render the page in

rather than quirks mode. Quirks mode is a compatibility setting browsers use for very old pages written before HTML standards stabilized — it causes subtle layout and CSS differences that make modern code behave unexpectedly. Without
, some browsers fall back to quirks mode.
declaration is not an HTML tag and has no closing counterpart. It is case-insensitive, but the convention is all-uppercase
tag — even before any whitespace or comments.
Older HTML specifications required longer, more complex DOCTYPE strings referencing a DTD (Document Type Definition). HTML5 simplified this to the short
. If you see a DOCTYPE like
, that is a legacy document — modern pages should use the HTML5 form.
element is the root of the document. Every other element is a descendant of it. It wraps exactly two children:

attribute is important and is frequently omitted. It tells browsers, search engines, and assistive technologies what language the page content is written in. Screen readers use it to select the correct text-to-speech voice. Search engines use it for language-specific indexing. Always set it to the primary language of your content (
for right-to-left languages like Arabic or Hebrew, or leave it unset (defaults to left-to-right).
Individual sections of the page can override
if they contain content in a different language:

This paragraph is in English.

Ce paragraphe est en français.

— information about the document that the browser and external services need, but the user does not see directly in the viewport. This includes character encoding, page title, viewport settings, linked stylesheets, and instructions for search engines, social media scrapers, and browser extensions.
This must be the first element inside
, and it must appear within the first 1024 bytes of the document:

UTF-8 is the correct encoding for almost every modern webpage. It covers the full Unicode character set — including emoji, Chinese, Arabic, accented Latin characters, and mathematical symbols. Without this tag, the browser must guess the encoding, which can cause garbled text (commonly called mojibake) for any characters outside basic ASCII.
UTF-8 must also match the Content-Type HTTP header your server sends (
). If they disagree, the browser may follow the HTTP header, making the meta tag ineffective for pages served over HTTP.
This tag controls how the browser scales the page on mobile devices:
Without this tag, mobile browsers default to rendering the page as if it were 980px wide, then zooming out to fit the screen. This makes most responsive CSS layouts look tiny and zoomed-out.
instructs the browser to set the viewport width equal to the device's physical screen width.
sets the initial zoom level to 100% (no zoom) on page load.

to the viewport meta tag. These prevent users from zooming in on content, which is a significant accessibility barrier for users with low vision. The WCAG 2.1 accessibility standard (Success Criterion 1.4.4) requires that text can be resized up to 200% without loss of content or functionality.

The title appears in the browser tab, in bookmarks, and as the clickable link text in search engine results. Each page of your application should have a distinct, descriptive title. A common pattern is
). The recommended length for search engine display is 50–60 characters — longer titles are truncated in results.

<!-- Load a CSS file -->
<link rel="stylesheet" href="/styles/global.css">

<!-- Defer: downloads in parallel, executes after HTML is parsed -->
<script src="/scripts/analytics.js" defer></script>

<!-- Async: downloads in parallel, executes as soon as downloaded -->
<script src="/scripts/third-party.js" async></script>

<!-- Preconnect to a third-party origin (reduces latency for fonts, APIs) -->
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
tag is void (self-closing) and used primarily for stylesheets and preconnects. Scripts in
as soon as downloaded, out of order
— the text snippet displayed under the link in search engine results. Keep under ~160 characters.
— Open Graph title displayed when this URL is shared on social media (Facebook, LinkedIn, etc.).
— the preview image shown when the URL is shared on social media.
— controls how Twitter (X) renders link previews.
— the small browser tab icon. Also use
— signals the authoritative URL to search engines, preventing duplicate-content penalties when a page is accessible via multiple URLs.
— tells crawlers to index the page and follow its links. Use
to exclude pages like admin dashboards.
element contains everything the user sees and interacts with. Structurally, a well-organized body uses semantic HTML5 elements to communicate the role of each region:

Home Blog About

Page Title

Content...

    ...

© 2025 My Company. All rights reserved.
Semantic elements convey meaning about the content they hold:
— introductory content for a section or the whole page. Typically contains the site logo, navigation, and a hero/banner.
— a set of navigation links. Use the
— the primary content of the document. A page should have exactly one
— self-contained content that could stand alone (a blog post, a news article, a forum post, a product card).
— a thematic grouping of content, typically with its own heading.
— content that is tangentially related to the surrounding content (a sidebar, a pull quote, an advert).
— closing content for a section or the page (copyright, links, contact info). Can appear inside
as well as at the page level.
for layout is valid, but screen readers, search engines, and browser reading-mode parsers all benefit from semantic structure. Use semantics where the meaning is clear and fall back to
Putting it all together, this is the minimal correct HTML5 template — the baseline every page starts from:

Home

Hello, world!

Page content goes here.

© 2025

In a Next.js project you will rarely write this file directly — Next.js generates it at build time or request time. However, knowing the expected shape helps you debug rendering anomalies and understand what the framework controls on your behalf.
values that tell the browser to fetch resources before they are needed, improving perceived load performance:

<!-- preconnect: open the TCP/TLS connection early (for third-party origins) -->
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>

<!-- preload: fetch this resource NOW, high priority, current page needs it -->
<link rel="preload" href="/fonts/inter.woff2" as="font" type="font/woff2" crossorigin>
<link rel="preload" href="/styles/critical.css" as="style">

<!-- prefetch: fetch this resource when idle, for a FUTURE navigation -->
<link rel="prefetch" href="/about" as="document">
is required — it tells the browser the resource type so it can set the correct request priority and headers. Omitting
causes the browser to fetch the resource with default priority, defeating the purpose of preloading. Incorrect
values cause the preloaded resource to be fetched twice (once by the preload, once when referenced by CSS/JS).
hints for fonts and critical CSS when you use
or import CSS files. You rarely need to add these manually, but understanding them helps you audit performance and interpret the rendered
How Next.js Manages the HTML Document

Per-Page Metadata in the App Router
Each page (or nested layout) in the App Router can export its own
object. Next.js merges metadata from all layouts in the route hierarchy and injects the result into
function can be async and can fetch data from APIs or databases. This generates correct
, Open Graph, and Twitter Card meta tags for every page without any raw HTML templating. At no point do you write
or manage the shell yourself — that is the job of
— machine-readable metadata embedded in a page that enables rich results (star ratings, FAQ dropdowns, recipe cards, event listings). The standard format is JSON-LD, embedded in a
In Next.js App Router you can also inject JSON-LD via the
approach shown above is more explicit and works in both App Router and Pages Router. Note the use of
— this is one of the rare cases where it is acceptable because the content is JSON you control, not user input.
Missing or wrong viewport meta tag

<!-- ❌ Missing viewport — mobile browsers render at 980px width and zoom out -->
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>My Page</title>
</head>

<!-- ✅ Always include viewport -->
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
  <title>My Page</title>
</head>
Render-blocking scripts in head without defer

<!-- ❌ Blocks HTML parsing until the script downloads and executes -->
<head>
  <script src="/heavy-library.js"></script>
</head>

<!-- ✅ defer: downloads in parallel, executes after HTML is parsed -->
<head>
  <script src="/heavy-library.js" defer></script>
</head>

<!-- ❌ Screen readers cannot determine the correct voice/language -->
<html>

<!-- ✅ Declare the primary document language -->
<html lang="en">
Adding html, head, or body tags inside a Next.js page
Using hooks or browser APIs in _document.tsx
Forgetting charset must be first in head

<!-- ❌ charset is declared after content that could be misread -->
<head>
  <title>My Page — Ünïcödé!</title>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">  <!-- too late for some browsers -->
</head>

<!-- ✅ charset must be within the first 1024 bytes, ideally first -->
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>My Page — Ünïcödé!</title>
</head>
: charset (UTF-8), viewport, title, description, icons, and stylesheets. Nothing in
is rendered visually in the viewport.

is required for accessibility — screen readers use it to select the correct voice and pronunciation rules.
Always include the viewport meta tag (
